points: Two lines intersect at exactly one point, forming 4 angles; adjacent angles (a linear pair) always sum to 180°, and opposite angles (vertically opposite) are always equal — proved by reasoning, not measurement.

Perpendicular lines are two lines that intersect at exactly 90°; all four angles formed are right angles, marked with a small square symbol. Parallel lines are two lines on the same plane that never meet, maintaining a constant perpendicular distance, marked with arrow symbols.

Parallel lines must lie on the same plane — two lines in different planes that never meet are NOT parallel; daily examples of parallel lines include opposite edges of a ruled notebook and railway tracks.
A transversal is a line crossing two other lines, forming 8 angles (4 at each intersection); due to vertically opposite pairs being equal, there are at most 4 distinct angle measures among the 8.

Corresponding angles occupy the same position (corner) at each intersection of a transversal; the corresponding angles test is both necessary and sufficient for parallelism: corresponding angles are equal if and only if the lines are parallel.

To draw a line parallel to a given line through a point, slide a set square along a ruler ensuring equal corresponding angles (90°), or fold two successive perpendiculars through the same point — both guarantee equal corresponding angles, confirming parallelism.

Alternate angles lie on opposite sides of the transversal, one at each intersection and between the two lines; they are always equal when the lines are parallel — proved using corresponding angles and vertically opposite angles together.
Co-interior (interior) angles lie on the same side of the transversal, between two parallel lines; they always add up to 180° — proved by combining linear pairs with corresponding angle equality.

All three angle relationships (corresponding = equal, alternate = equal, co-interior = 180°) are equivalent tests for parallel lines and can be used to find unknown angles in figures with parallel lines cut by a transversal.
